question: In the Cheng 2017 study on synthesis of nanocrystalline TaC powders via high temperature spray pyrolysis, what mass of phenolic resin was used in recipe R1?
reference_answer: Recipe R1 used 0.138 grams of phenolic resin as the carbon precursor in the HTSP synthesis method.
evidence_any: for recipe R1, phenolic resin was first dissolved in ethanol to form an orange-colored solution
Table 1 Summary of recipes for different mixed solutions used for the synthesis of nanocrystalline TaC powders
Recipe R1 0.330 tantalum chloride g 0.138 phenolic resin g
excerpts: In particular, for the synthesis of nanocrystalline TaC UHTC powders via the single-step HTSP process, the starting tantalum precursor used is tantalum pentachloride (TaCl5, 99.8%, Alfa Aesar #14164), the carbon precursor used is Novolac phenolic resin (Plenco, 14353, R5420), and the solvents used are absolute ethanol (99.5+%, Acros Organics #61509-0020) or 1-pentanol (99+%, Alfa Aesar # 30898). Two recipes were adopted, as summarized in Table 1: for recipe R1, phenolic resin was first dissolved in ethanol to form an orange-colored solution. TaCl5 was dissolved separately in 1-pentanol, into which the phenolic resin-ethanol solution was then added. For recipe R2, ethanol was used as the solvent for both phenolic resin and TaCl5.

question: According to Table 1 in Cheng et al. 2017, what was the C:Ta2O5 molar ratio specified for recipe R2 in the nanocrystalline TaC synthesis?
reference_answer: Recipe R2 was designed with a C:Ta2O5 molar ratio of 9:1, calculated assuming phenolic resin gives a carbon yield of 40 wt% after thermolysis.
evidence_any: Table 1 Summary of recipes for different mixed solutions used for the synthesis
Recipe R2 0.796 tantalum chloride g 0.300 phenolic resin g C:Ta2O5 molar ratioa 9: 1
excerpts: Two recipes were adopted, as summarized in Table 1: for recipe R1, phenolic resin was first dissolved in ethanol to form an orange-colored solution. TaCl5 was dissolved separately in 1-pentanol, into which the phenolic resin-ethanol solution was then added. For recipe R2, ethanol was used as the solvent for both phenolic resin and TaCl5. The precursor solutions were either used directly for HTSP as carried out for R1, or mixed with a small amount of ammonia solution (2 M solution in ethanol, Sigma-Aldrich #392685) to remove Cl ion as precipitated NH4Cl crystals from the precursor solution and then used for HTSP as carried out for R2.

question: In the You et al. 2025 work on 2D SiC/SiC composites, what was the average crack spacing measured at room temperature according to Table 2?
reference_answer: The average crack spacing at room temperature was 26.89 micrometers with a variance of 7.76, measured from periodically distributed matrix cracks.
evidence_any: Table 2 Variation of crack spacing with temperature T /oC Crack spacing/μm Average/μm
Quantitative characterization from Fig. 7a revealed periodically distributed matrix cracks
excerpts: Quantitative characterization from Fig. 7a revealed periodically distributed matrix cracks adjacent to fracture surface, with statistically analyzed crack spacing data presented in Table 2. reference_answer: The measured tensile modulus at 1500 degrees Celsius was 199.60 GPa, compared to a calculated value of 171.50 GPa with an error of 8.52%.

question: According to Table 4 in You et al. 2025, what was the measured matrix cracking stress at room temperature in the 2D SiC/SiC composites?
reference_answer: The measured matrix cracking stress (sigma_mc) at room temperature was 139.30 megapascals, slightly higher than the calculated value of 115.78 megapascals with an error of 5.51%.
evidence_any: Table 4 Comparison between calculated and measured values of sigma_mc
σmc could be effectively predicted based on interphase parameters obtained from push-in tests
excerpts: According to Evans et al., the matrix cracking stress (σmc) exhibited a strong correlation with interphase parameters (τc, Γi), with their quantitative relationship characterized by Eq. (7). Consequently, σmc could be effectively predicted based on interphase parameters obtained from push-in tests. Furthermore, employing the proportional limit stress (PLS) theory proposed by Liu et al., the experimentally measured values could be equivalently represented by PLS. The final comparison between measured and calculated values was presented in Table 4. Table 4 Comparison between calculated and measured values of σmc T/℃ Calculated values/MPa Measured values/MPa Error/% As received 115.78 139.30 5.51% 1000℃ 155.41 155.60 12.24% 1250℃ 115.48 121.70 19.24% 1350℃ 130.36 144.60 29.25% 1500℃ 130.18 128.30 6.28%

question: In the You 2025 study, what was the measured tensile strength of 2D SiC/SiC composites at 1000 degrees Celsius according to Table 5?
reference_answer: The measured tensile strength at 1000 degrees Celsius was 300.25 megapascals, showing good agreement with the calculated value of 328.81 megapascals with a 1.66% error.
evidence_any: Table 5 Comparison between calculated and measured values of σp T/℃ 1000℃
σp similarly demonstrated significant temperature-dependent degradation
excerpts: As presented in Table 5, σp similarly demonstrated significant temperature-dependent degradation. The prediction errors remained within 30% across the entire temperature range from room temperature to 1500°C, indicating good overall reliability of the predictive model. The primary sources of prediction error originate from, dynamic variations in fiber volume fraction during high-temperature tensile loading, and the influence of test region selection on characteristic length measurements. Table 5 Comparison between calculated and measured values of σp T/℃ Calculated values/MPa Measured values/MPa Error/% As received 303.03 397.48 17.70% 1000℃ 328.81 300.25 1.66% 1250℃ 286.86 248.44 2.23% 1350℃ 299.00 204.26 -28.40% 1500℃ 313.35 287.90 0.07%

reference_answer: The measured tensile strength at 1250 degrees Celsius was 248.44 megapascals, with a calculated value accounting for stress-oxidation of 221.54 megapascals and an error of 10.83%.

question: In the Cheng 2017 TaC synthesis study, what volume of 1-pentanol was used in recipe R1 according to Table 1?
reference_answer: Recipe R1 required 2.840 grams of 1-pentanol as one of the solvents for dissolving the tantalum chloride precursor.
evidence_any: Table 1 Summary of recipes for different mixed solutions Recipe R1 1-Pentanol g 2.840
TaCl5 was dissolved separately in 1-pentanol, into which the phenolic resin-ethanol solution was then added
the solvents used are absolute ethanol or 1-pentanol
excerpts: In particular, for the synthesis of nanocrystalline TaC UHTC powders via the single-step HTSP process, the starting tantalum precursor used is tantalum pentachloride (TaCl5, 99.8%, Alfa Aesar #14164), the carbon precursor used is Novolac phenolic resin (Plenco, 14353, R5420), and the solvents used are absolute ethanol (99.5+%, Acros Organics #61509-0020) or 1-pentanol (99+%, Alfa Aesar # 30898). Two recipes were adopted, as summarized in Table 1: for recipe R1, phenolic resin was first dissolved in ethanol to form an orange-colored solution. TaCl5 was dissolved separately in 1-pentanol, into which the phenolic resin-ethanol solution was then added. For recipe R2, ethanol was used as the solvent for both phenolic resin and TaCl5.

question: According to Table 2 in You et al. 2025, what was the average crack spacing at 1500 degrees Celsius in the 2D SiC/SiC composites?
reference_answer: The average crack spacing at 1500 degrees Celsius was 17.02 micrometers with a variance of 4.934, representing the smallest average spacing across all tested temperatures.

periodically distributed matrix cracks adjacent to fracture surface were observed
Quantitative characterization from Fig. 7a revealed crack spacing data presented in Table 2
